unitTitle: GCSE Programming part 4 — subroutines (Teach Computing — adapted)

lessons.title: Subroutines — parameters and decomposition
lessons.objectives:
I can describe what a subroutine is
I can explain what parameters and arguments are
I can write a subroutine that uses parameters
I can use subroutines to break a problem into parts
lessons.outline:
Routine first (same every lesson). Vocabulary on board: subroutine · parameter · argument · decomposition. TA: everyone has Python (Trinket or IDE) open. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — predict the calculate(a, b) program on the calculator-program image (think–pair–share). Pupils PREDICT the output (no writing for Support — tick-boxes).
   • Support: tick what a subroutine is / which line is the call.  • Core: write the output.  • Challenge: name what a and b are called inside.
2. (5–12) I-do → we-do — define a subroutine, then arguments vs parameters. Stress: the value is COPIED in (misconception M24 — no live link between the argument and the parameter).
   • Support: order the Parson’s puzzle that doubles a number.  • Core: name the arguments / explain the copy.  • Challenge: write a highest(a, b) subroutine too. Likely error: forgetting to CALL the subroutine so nothing happens; fix-words “call it by name on a new line.”
3. (12–35) You-do — pair programming (driver / navigator, swap every 5 mins): write average_value(a, b, c) that prints the average of three numbers.
   • Support: the Parson’s puzzle.  • Core: write it from the worked example.  • Challenge: add a second subroutine. TA: “prompt, do not do it for them.” They paste their link + a screenshot.
4. (35–50) Decomposition — break a calculator into small subroutines (card-sort: which part is a subroutine, which belongs in the main program). Pairs decompose a similar program.
5. (50–55) Plenary — pupils self-tick the 4 “I can…”; list advantages of subroutines (less repetition, easier to read/fix, reusable). TA notes who used parameters.
Differentiation is on the SAME task, low floor / high ceiling — nobody’s screen looks “less done”. No flashing/sound on slides; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Functions — return values and trace tables
lessons.objectives:
I can explain the difference between a function and a procedure
I can read a function that uses return
I can use a trace table to investigate a function
I can write a function that returns a value
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: function · procedure · return value · trace table. TA: everyone has Python open. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — predict the powers(a, b) program. Most predict “8”; it actually CRASHES (“answer is not defined”) because answer only exists inside the function.
   • Support: tick whether the last line can use answer / which word returns a value.  • Core: explain the crash.  • Challenge: explain how return + storing the value fixes it.
2. (5–17) I-do → we-do — return sends the VALUE back; store it to keep it. Live-code adding return (demonstration — pupils watch). Card-sort function vs procedure.
   • Support: card-sort.  • Core: name a difference.  • Challenge: a built-in like int() is a function.
3. (17–32) We-do — trace tables on the trace-table image. Fill to_the_power(2, 3) together (one row at a time), then pupils trace find_highest(9, 12). NOTE: the engine has no interactive trace grid yet — pupils use the embedded screenshot + fill-blank/choice cells; logged in notes (§2.7).
4. (32–50) You-do — write average_value(a, b) that RETURNS the average; store and print it.
   • Support: choice cells.  • Core: trace + write.  • Challenge: area_circle(radius) that returns the area. Likely error: printing inside instead of returning; fix-words “return it, don’t print it.” They screenshot their program.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the 4 “I can…”; one difference between a function and a procedure. TA notes who wrote a function that returns.
Differentiation on the SAME task; movement breaks routine; no flashing/sound.

resources.title: trace table for a function (Teach Computing).png

lessons.title: Scope — local, global and constants
lessons.objectives:
I can describe what local and global scope mean
I can spot whether a variable is local or global
I can change a global program to pass values through parameters
I can describe what a constant is
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: scope · local · global · parameter · constant. TA: everyone has Python open. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — predict the output of the example() program (two variables both called number). It prints 10 (the local one), not 5 (the global one).
   • Support: tick local vs global.  • Core: explain why it prints 10.  • Challenge: why changing the local does not change the global.
2. (5–20) I-do → we-do — scope is what can be seen (telescope image). Step through three examples: reading a global; a local shadowing a global; using global to actually change it. Stress global is bad practice — prefer parameters.
   • Support: card-sort local vs global.  • Core: why parameters are better.  • Challenge: convert the score program.
3. (20–45) You-do — “not going global”: rewrite a global program (double) to pass num1 in as a parameter and return it.
   Likely error: forgetting to store the returned value; fix-words “catch it: num1 = double(num1).” TA: “prompt, do not do it for them.” They screenshot the program.
4. (45–52) Constants — a value that should not change; Python uses CAPITALS (LIVES = 5). Convention only.
5. (52–55) Plenary — self-tick the 4 “I can…”; one reason global variables are risky. (Optional lesson quiz on local/global lines.) TA notes who used parameters instead of global.
Differentiation on the SAME task; movement breaks routine; no flashing/sound.

lessons.title: Making an XOR function — truth tables
lessons.objectives:
I can read and complete a truth table
I can describe what XOR does
I can design an XOR function in pseudocode
I can create and test an XOR function
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: XOR · operator · truth table · Boolean · return value. TA: everyone has Python open. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–7) Starter — AND/OR recap: peer-instruction predict the output of an and condition, then reveal. Recap AND (both) vs OR (at least one).
   • Support: tick when AND / OR is True.  • Core: change the condition and explain.  • Challenge: predict how XOR differs.
2. (7–20) I-do → we-do — truth tables (AND table image): 0 = False, 1 = True. Complete the first AND row together; pupils complete AND and OR tables by editing and running code.
3. (20–25) Introduce XOR — True when the inputs are DIFFERENT, False when the SAME. Fill the XOR truth table together (multi-choice cells). Label-the-parts task on the AND-function image revises subroutine name / parameters / return value / arguments.
4. (25–50) You-do — design the XOR function in pseudocode (Parson’s puzzle), then write and test it against the truth table.
   • Support: order the pseudocode.  • Core: complete the table + label.  • Challenge: use the XOR function inside an if. Likely error: getting the same/different logic backwards; fix-words “XOR is True when a and b are DIFFERENT.” They screenshot the function.
5. (50–55) Plenary — “what is the output?” for a few calls (True/False on mini whiteboards); self-tick the 4 “I can…”.
Differentiation on the SAME task; movement breaks routine; no flashing/sound.

lessons.title: Structured programming — one way in, one way out
lessons.objectives:
I can explain "one entry point, one exit point"
I can improve code to remove break and extra returns
I can read a structure chart
I can complete a program from a structure chart
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: structured programming · entry point · exit point · interface · structure chart. TA: everyone has Python open. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — count the exit points in an XOR-style function (two returns). The term may be new; point to each return. Structured code has ONE exit point.
   • Support: tick how many entry/exit points a block should have.  • Core: why two returns breaks the rule.  • Challenge: how to make one return at the end.
2. (5–20) I-do → we-do — the structured approach: one entry, one exit; avoid break and multiple returns; stop a loop by setting its condition False. Worked “improve the code” example.
   • Support: card-sort follows / breaks the structured approach.  • Core: rewrite multiple_five with one return.  • Challenge: improve the password program.
3. (20–30) Why structure it — GOTO “spaghetti code”; blocks let a team split the work; planning first reduces build time.
4. (30–50) You-do — structure charts (dog-walking invoice image). Read the chart (identifier · parameters · return), then complete the dog-walking invoice program: walks = dogs × days, cost = walks × 4.00.
   Likely error: a subroutine that prints instead of returning; fix-words “return it so the next subroutine can use it.” TA: “prompt, do not do it for them.” They screenshot the program.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the 4 “I can…”; one advantage of the structured approach. TA notes who removed extra returns / completed a subroutine from the chart.
Differentiation on the SAME task; movement breaks routine; no flashing/sound.

lessons.title: Create a program — testing and a final project (double lesson)
lessons.objectives:
I can describe iterative testing and final testing
I can sort data into erroneous, boundary, and normal
I can plan a program from a structure chart
I can create and test a program
lessons.outline:
Double lesson and a project — take your time. Routine first. Vocabulary on board: iterative testing · final testing · erroneous · boundary · normal. TA: learning partners; check in every 15 minutes. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — what is iterative testing? Testing as you build (e.g. add(3, 4) to check a function). They already do it; now name it.
   • Support: tick iterative vs final.  • Core: why test as you go.  • Challenge: which testing was add(3, 4)?
2. (5–20) I-do → we-do — final testing with three data types: normal, boundary, erroneous (card-sort). A test table records test · input · expected · pass/fail. Complete a row together.
3. (20–35) Introduce the project — Copington theme park ticket machine (scenario image). Welcome, ask for adult/child/senior tickets, total the cost, print a ticket. Structure chart provided (entrance, wristband, collect, issue_ticket). Reassure: biggest program so far — “do your best.”
4. (35–95) Design → create → test (you do) — plan pseudocode for the entrance subroutine first, then build, testing as you go. Learning partners discuss every 15 mins (feedback journal). The collect subroutine (counting £10/£20 notes) is hardest — support there.
   • Support: read/plan one subroutine + the test table.  • Core: build entrance + total the cost with validation.  • Challenge: explorer tasks (keep running, capacity limit, admin code). Likely error: coding with no plan; fix-words “design one subroutine, build it, test it, then the next.” Assess with the rubric. They screenshot the working machine.
5. (95–110) Plenary + unit quiz — self-tick the 4 “I can…”, then the Subroutines unit quiz (key words, sort the test data, function multi-select, fill the gaps, write/improve code, confidence slider).
Differentiation on the SAME task; movement breaks routine; no flashing/sound.
